## [00:00 - 03:03]

Now let's look at diagnostics. So with diagnostics, we want to check with the pixel to see if any errors have come back or Facebook have highlighted any concerns. So again, in the events manager section, find your pixel and then at the top click on diagnostics. Keep an eye on this. So one of the challenges with privacy changes that Facebook has been forced to make is also stopping you from sending data in plain text that can be identifiable as far as users concerned. So that could include email address and phone number and anything else that's passed in the URL. So Facebook sees that in the URL, it will reject it, it will highlight a concern, and it's a violation. Violation means that Facebook could end up and this is just an FYI, just to make sure you're careful, it could end up with your your account being banned, for example, if it's if it's a really bad set, if it's a consistent problem, and Facebook have seen this come through a lot, then you could potentially end up with an ad account ban if Facebook decide that's the the route they want to take. So if you see violating conditions, expand it out and see where it's occurring and what you can do to change it. So for example, on this particular store, on this pixel, the initiate checkout and page for your events, the URL queries are sending the customer email, and it's sending it in plain text. So Facebook can actually see that. So I've clicked here, see the see details and affected URLs. And you can see here in the URL where it's been highlighted in the black and I can't hover over it because I'll lose that there, that the URL has customer ID equals removed customer email equals removed email equals removed, Facebook have removed those, because it's it protects their own interests from having personalized, personalized, personally identifiable customer information sent to them. When that data is sent by the conversion API, or any other methods, it has to be hashed. Hashed means that that data is stored against a secure key. And then Facebook cannot see that individual data, but they use the key to identify it. So if you pass through your URL, you know, email equals john at Smith.com or something, then you know, that doesn't work. So this often happens when you're using third party apps. Now this website and this business in particular, I know that they're using a third party app. I think it's cart hook in particular that does pass this information in the URL. This is a problem. And not to call out any particular app is there are many apps that do this. But be careful, because at the end of the day, you don't want to lose your pixel, your ad account, I and therefore your pixel as well, based on something

## [03:03 - 03:45]

that is in your control to fix. So in most cases, you know, you won't encounter this if you've got a standard Shopify setup conversion API and things like that. But as you start to add apps, and you start to customize, whether you're on Shopify or any other system, just keep an eye on this, it doesn't need more than a weekly check, you don't have to come in every day, every hour, or at least once a week, just come in and check for diagnostics. You can decide to ignore things. So for example, same event ID received. This one's been ignored. This one you could say that I'm going to click here and ignore. I would not ignore this one. This one's a bit more serious. So just keep a close eye on diagnostic.
